data.title: US trade deficit narrows in January as exports jump to record high
data.description:
The U.S. trade deficit narrowed sharply in January as exports surged to a record high and imports fell, a trend that if sustained, could see trade contributing to economic growth in the first quarter.

The trade gap contracted 25.3% to $54.5 billion, the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis and Census Bureau said on Thursday. Data for December was revised to show the deficit widening to $72.9 billion instead of $70.3 billion as previously estimated. Economists polled by Reuters forecast the trade deficit shrinking to $66.6 billion in January.

The report was delayed because of last year's government shutdown. Trade data have been volatile amid President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs. The import duties, which Trump pursued under a law meant for use in national emergencies, were struck down by the U.S. Supreme Court.

But Trump responded to the ruling by imposing a 10% global tariff, which he said would rise to 15%. The Trump administration said on Wednesday it was launching two trade investigations into excess industrial capacity in 16 major trading partners and into forced labor.

Trump has defended the tariffs as necessary to address trade imbalances and protect U.S. industries. So far, the manufacturing rebirth has not materialized and 100,000 factory jobs have been lost since January 2025.

Exports jumped 5.5% to an all-time high of $302.1 billion in January. The increase was the largest since October 2021. Goods exports soared 8.1% to $195.5 billion. They were boosted by a $9.4 billion increase in exports of industrial supplies and materials, mostly nonmonetary gold and other precious metals.

Capital goods exports increased $5.4 billion to a record high, boosted by computers, civilian aircraft as well as computer accessories. Exports of other goods rose $2.9 billion, also to an all-time high. But consumer goods exports decreased $2.8 billion to the lowest level since October 2022, pulled down by a $2.1 billion decline in pharmaceutical preparations.

Imports fell 0.7% to $356.6 billion in January. Goods imports slipped 1.0% to $277.3 billion. They were dragged down by a $3.3 billion decrease in consumer goods, mostly pharmaceutical preparations. Imports of automotive vehicles, parts and engines fell $2.8 billion amid decreases in trucks, buses and special-purpose vehicles, as well as passenger cars.

Imports of industrial supplies and materials dropped $1.4 billion, with nonmonetary gold falling $1.1 billion.

But imports of capital goods increased $3.4 billion to a record high, driven by computers and telecommunications equipment, likely linked to artificial intelligence and the construction of data centers.

The goods trade deficit narrowed 17.6% to $81.8 billion in January. Exports of services increased $1.2 billion to a record high $106.7 billion, reflecting rises in other business services, financial services and charges for the use of intellectual property. Exports of travel services fell $0.3 billion, likely reflecting a reduction in tourism.

Imports of services increased $0.2 billion to an all-time high of $79.3 billion amid gains in other business services and insurance services.

Trade made a negligible contribution to the economy's 1.4% annualized growth pace in the October-December quarter.

data.title: Oil tops $101 before dipping as Iran ramps up strikes and IEA calls conflict biggest supply disruption in history
data.description:
By Nora Redmond

Three cargo ships attacked in the Persian Gulf by Iran

Brent crude touched $101 before retreating as Iran stepped up its attack on Gulf infrastructures.

Oil prices spiked again on Thursday after Iran ramped up its strikes on infrastructure in the Gulf and as the International Energy Agency said the conflict has caused a historical disruption of supply.

West Texas Intermediate crude futures (CL.1) (CLJ26) were climbing 7% to $93.40 a barrel, while Brent crude (BRN00) (BRNK26) was advancing by 7.3% to trade at $98.65. Brent oil touched a high of $101.25 overnight before retreating to below the $100 threshold.

The renewed climb comes after at least three cargo ships were attacked in the Persian Gulf by Iran, bringing the total to six vessels across just two days. Oman was forced to evacuate all of its ships from its main export ?terminal at Mina ?Al ?Fahal, and two Iraqi tankers were struck offshore, forcing its terminals to suspend operations, according to a Bloomberg report on Thursday.

"The war in the Middle East is creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market," the IEA said in its March report released on Thursday. Disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz have caused Gulf countries to cut total oil production by at least 10 million barrels per day (mb/d), the energy body added.

The IEA expects the global supply of oil to fall by 8 million barrels per day in March, with production cuts partially offset by higher output from non-OPEC+ members Kazakhstan and Russia.

Widespread flight cancellations, with thousands canceled across the region, disruptions to liquefied petroleum gas supplies and a precarious global economic outlook have led to the agency reducing its estimates for the global consumption of oil by around 25% to 640,000 barrels a day.

See: Why the IEA's largest-ever release of oil reserves is pushing up crude prices

Concerns from traders have risen despite the IEA on Wednesday announcing that its 32 member countries had unanimously agreed to release 400 million barrels of oil from its emergency reserves - marking the largest amount ever to be made available.

Iran has significantly ramped up its efforts in the Middle East conflict. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps also reportedly threatened to target "economic centers and banks belonging to the U.S. and the Zionist regime in the region."

The change in strategy from Tehran has spooked investors, with fears over prolonged disruption to supplies.

"The problem is that investors are increasingly pricing in a more protracted conflict that causes extensive economic damage," Jim Reid, global head of macro research and thematic strategy at Deutsche Bank, wrote in a note.

"After all, with no concrete signs of de-escalation yet, that's keeping oil prices elevated, and raising the risk of a broader stagflationary shock," he said.

Isabel Wang contributed

-Nora Redmond

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US forecaster sees La Niña ending soon, El Niño likely by mid-2026
data.description:
La Niña is expected to shift to El Nino-Southern Oscillation‑neutral conditions in the coming month, with neutral conditions with 55% chances through the May–July period, the U.S. Climate Prediction Center said on Thursday.

The U.S. weather forecaster added that El Nino has a 62% chance of developing between June and August 2026 and is expected to continue through at least the end of 2026.

"The increasing odds of El Niño are supported by the large amount of heat in the subsurface ocean and the expected weakening of the low-level trade winds," the CPC added.

  • La Niña is part of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation climatic cycle, which affects water temperatures in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean.

  • La Niña results in cooler water temperatures, increasing the chance of floods and drought, which can impact crops. When ENSO is neutral, water temperatures stay around the average level, leading to more stable weather and potentially better crop yields.

  • "ENSO is transitioning to neutral, but weak La Nina conditions persist," said Jason Nicholls, lead international forecaster at AccuWeather.

  • "A cooling of water temperatures off western Indonesia, in the southeast tropical Indian Ocean, will contribute to drier conditions this spring and summer."

  • Donald Keeney, agricultural meteorologist at Vaisala Weather, said neutral conditions are expected through spring and early summer, with El Niño possibly developing later and this should bring wetter conditions in Argentina and drier weather in central Brazil.

  • "(El Nino's) impact on global agriculture will be largely determined by the associated weather patterns that El Nino brings. The emergence of El Nino poses a downside risk to several commodities," said Matthew Biggin, senior analyst at BMI, a Fitch Solutions company.

  • Japan's weather bureau said Tuesday there was a 60% chance of El Niño emerging this summer.

  • Indonesia expects a longer and more severe dry season in 2026, beginning in April and peaking in August, its meteorology agency said, raising the likelihood of drought.

data.title: Irish Inflation Remains Stable At 2.7%
data.description:
DUBLIN (dpa-AFX) - Ireland's consumer price inflation held steady in February after easing in the previous two months, data from the Central Statistics Office showed on Thursay.

The consumer price index climbed 2.7 percent year-over-year in February, the same as in the previous month. Moreover, a similar inflation rate was last seen in September 2025.

The annual price growth in food and non-alcoholic beverages eased to 3.3 percent from 3.9 percent. Inflation based on clothing and footwear moderated to 5.7 percent from 7.3 percent, while transport charges continued to decrease by 0.3 percent.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices rebounded 0.9 percent after falling 0.9 percent in January.

The EU measure of inflation, the harmonized index of consumer prices, also climbed at a stable pace of 2.5 percent annually in February. Monthly, the HICP increased 0.8 percent.

data.title: Iran’s new supreme leader vows to keep Strait of Hormuz blocked
data.description:
In the first public statement since his appointment, he also said that all US bases in the Middle East should be closed and would be attacked.

In comments being read out on Iranian state media, Mojtaba - who succeeds his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei - said the vital Strait of Hormuz should remain closed as a "tool to pressure the enemy".

"Iran will not refrain from avenging the blood of its martyrs," he said, adding that Iran will seek compensation from enemies "or destroy their assets accordingly".

data.title: Costco Sues Government, Customer Sues Costco. Tariff Refunds Are Getting Messy. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

When the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump's tariffs last month, the development was expected to be a boon to retailers like Costco Wholesale. That may not be the case.

Costco on Wednesday became the first retailer to face a potential class-action lawsuit related to tariff refunds. A suit filed in Illinois federal court alleges that Costco owes its shoppers refunds in lieu of the Supreme Court's ruling against Trump's sweeping tariffs enacted under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

The suit was filed on behalf of a Costco member in the state, Matthew Stockov, and seeks class-action status, meaning it has the potential to snowball into something bigger.

"Even when the Supreme Court strikes down an unlawful tariff, the truly injured parties possess no direct avenue for redress," the suit read, in part. Costco has declined to comment on the lawsuit.

The warehouse club is among a handful of retailers seeking refunds from the Trump administration. Although the Supreme Court invalidated the emergency levies in its ruling, it provided no mechanism to deliver refunds of duties paid to eligible importers.

Costco sued the administration back in November, aiming to block the tariffs and secure refunds of taxes it already had paid. The company noted at the time that importers weren't guaranteed refunds without securing their own court judgment.

Speaking on the company's March 5 earnings call, CEO Ron Vachris indicated the situation was in flux. "It is not yet clear what the process will be, what refunds, if any, will be received, and when this will happen," Vachris said.

He asserted that Costco had countered the tariffs to the best of its ability over the past year, and "in many cases" didn't pass the full cost on to shoppers. Still, ever-changing rates and the layering of different taxes on top of each other "made it challenging to track the exact impact to an individual item sold," Vachris added.

Even if more clarity develops on this front, trade tensions are intensifying. The administration has imposed 10% tariffs on foreign-made goods under section 122 of the 1974 Trade Act, which expire on July 24. President Donald Trump vowed after the ruling to hike that rate to 15%.

Still, the ruling was seen as a major victory for importers. Ropes & Gray LLP, a New York-based law firm, noted in February that the decision represented "an opportunity to pursue potentially significant refunds."

More than 1,000 businesses had sought refunds beforehand, a number that likely will grow. An estimated $175 billion in collected tariffs that have been deemed illegal now hangs in the balance. However, the latest development for Costco indicates the already-murky path forward may be even more complicated.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Exclsuive | NSE guns a valuation of ₹5 lakh crore, sources say
data.description:
The world’s largest derivatives exchange — National Stock Exchange of India (NSE) — is eyeing a valuation of over ₹5 lakh crore as it moves ahead with its long-awaited listing, according to a person with direct knowledge of the matter.

The exchange is considering diluting 4–5% through an offer for sale (OFS) in its initial public offering (IPO). “We have around 1.9 lakh retail shareholders. As per Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) rules, any shareholder who has held shares for more than a year must be given the option to offer their shares for sale. We will also have to see how many large shareholders come forward to sell,” the person said.

This could make it India’s second or third-largest IPO, potentially raising ₹20,000–₹25,000 crore. India’s largest IPO so far has been that of Hyundai Motor India, worth ₹27,870 crore, followed by Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) at ₹21,008 crore.

In the grey market, NSE shares are trading at around ₹1,975 per share, implying a valuation of about ₹4.88 lakh crore at a price-to-earnings multiple of 40x.

NSE’s IPO has long been delayed due to the overhang of the co-location case. Earlier last year, the exchange set aside nearly ₹1,400 crore towards settling the matter with the markets regulator.

Earlier in the day, the exchange said it had appointed 20 merchant bankers for the IPO — the highest ever for an Indian public issue. Eight law firms have also been selected to advise on regulatory, documentation and compliance aspects. The IPO committee, chaired by Srinivas Injeti, approved the appointments following a structured, transparent and competitive evaluation process.

data.title: Palantir CEO Alex Karp Highlights Faster, More Precise Warfare Enabled By AI Platforms Against Iran
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. CEO Alex Karp highlighted the transformative role of the company's AI platforms in modern warfare, particularly in the United States' war against Iran in the Middle East. 

During an interview with CNBC on Thursday, he emphasized that technology improvements allow military forces to operate with unprecedented precision, speed, and coordination across multiple domains. Karp explained that tools like Palantir’s (PLTR) Project Maven form an important part of these capabilities.

“The fact that you can now target more precisely, quickly, and accurately, and organize the total power of our fleet and resources and bring it to bear against adversaries has shifted the way war is fought, and I have read that Palantir’s project maven is the core backbone of that.”

-Alex Karp, CEO, Palantir

Get updates to this developing story directly on Stocktwits.

data.title: Bumble shares surge as investors swipe right on AI-powered reboot
data.description:
By Kritika Lamba

Bumble shares jumped more than 40% in early trading on Thursday after the company posted upbeat fourth-quarter revenue and unveiled an AI‑driven overhaul of its apps to lure back younger users.

The rebound comes after years of losses and battered investor confidence, with the stock losing half of its value last year as growth in the online dating market slowed amid stiff competition.

CEO Whitney Wolfe Herd is betting that a revamped product could reinvigorate growth and appeal to younger users who complain of swiping fatigue.

The company is preparing to launch Bumble 2.0 that uses artificial intelligence to enhance quick photo swipes with a scrollable profile of short chapters that outline a user's interests, lifestyle and personality. Herd also said that Bumble could experiment with a "no‑swipe" experience in some markets.

Analysts, however, struck a cautious note on the degree to which the redesign would turn around Bumble's fortunes. They are watching for signs of "meaningful innovation" in an industry that has seen little change since the swipe‑based design became standard.

The dating category has had "multiple false starts", analysts at Jefferies said. "While early signs of stabilization are encouraging, we need to see a more sustained improvement to turn constructive."

Dating applications like Match Group's Hinge are also rolling out AI‑powered tools aimed at improving user experience to win back younger users as dating apps race to adapt to shifting preferences.

Bumble reported fourth-quarter revenue of $224.2 million, topping analysts' estimates of $221.3 million, while average revenue per paying user jumped 7.9% to $22.20. Its performance‑marketing spend dropped more than 80% year‑on‑year.

Raymond James analysts said near‑term momentum for Bumble still depends on stabilizing paid users and proving that the post‑reset ecosystem can grow without heavy reliance on paid acquisition.

The stock trades at 3.55 times its projected earnings for the next 12 months, compared with 11.05 times for the Match Group.

data.title: All the Ways Trump Has Said When the Iran War Will End — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Callum Keown

Investors need the answer to one big question right now — when will the Iran war end?

Fortunately, President Donald Trump and others in his administration have offered a running commentary on the timeline. Unfortunately, their remarks have left global markets none the wiser.

Goldman Sachs analysts have even been tracking the language used by officials to gain clues on the expected duration of the conflict, cataloging quotes from Trump and others in the administration in a recent note on oil.

The market has been moving on the specific language. Oil prices plunged and stocks rallied after Trump told CBS the war was "very complete," on Monday. However, Brent crude topped $100 a barrel again early Thursday as continued strikes in the Middle East suggested the conflict was far from over.

The phenomenon began shortly after the first U.S. attacks on Iran, when Trump said he expected the campaign to last four to five weeks, but added it could "go far longer than that."

Then on March 4, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth tried to clarify the timeline further. "We can say four weeks, but it could be six, it could be eight, it could be three," he said during a press conference.

Last week Energy Secretary Chris Wright attempted to put a timeline on when more regular flows of oil may resume through the Strait of Hormuz. "Worst case, that's a few weeks. That's not months," he told Fox News.

Then came Trump's "very complete, pretty much" remark, as reported by CBS. He later said the war would end "very soon," though not this week, during a press conference Monday.

There will undoubtedly be more examples in the coming days, weeks, or even months.

Rather than trying to make sense of words and phrases like "soon," "very soon," "very complete," and "few weeks," investors may be better off focusing on tangible developments in the Middle East — particularly around shipping through the Strait of Hormuz — or other concrete signs the conflict is nearing an end.

Write to Callum Keown at callum.keown@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dan Ives Is Stepping Down as Eightco Chairman — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Eightco Holdings announced Thursday that Dan Ives is stepping down as chairman of the company, whose shares have fallen more than 90% in recent months.

Ives, a prominent tech analyst at Wedbush, was named chairman of the digital currency treasury firm last September.

As part of the transition, Eightco announced $125 million in new funding commitments, including $75 million from BitMine, a digital asset company chaired by cryptocurrency bull Tom Lee. Cathie Wood's Ark Invest and Payward, the parent company of crypto platform Kraken, have also committed $25 million each.

Tom Lee is joining the company's board of directors.

Shares of Eightco were up 22%, to 99 cents, on Thursday morning.

"I am so excited about this next chapter for ORBS," Ives said in a statement. "The combination of Tom Lee, ARK Investment Management, Cathie Wood and CEO Kevin O'Donnell is the perfect team in my view to take the strategic vision next level."

Wedbush declined to comment.

In late January, Barron's published a cover story on Ives's overlapping roles in the world of technology and their potential to create conflicts of interest with his role as a sell-side analyst.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Soy, corn basis steady-mixed as farmer selling eases
data.description:
Basis bids for corn and soybeans were flat to mixed in the U.S. Midwest on Thursday as a wave of farmer selling that began early in the week petered out, though futures continued to rise.

  • Farmers had earlier taken advantage of a massive rally in agricultural commodities to forward contract new-crop corn, soy and wheat and sell off grains in storage.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported net weekly U.S. soybean export sales for 2025/26 for the week ended on March 5 at 466,200 metric tons, in line with analyst estimates.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported net weekly U.S. corn export sales for 2025/26 for the week ended on March 5 at 1,503,800 metric tons, on the low end of a range of analyst estimates.

  • Chicago soybean futures rose for a third consecutive session on Thursday, as crude oil climbed further following Iranian attacks that deepened fears of a protracted Middle East conflict.

  • Wheat and corn also rose, with the strength in crude again offsetting a backdrop of ample global grain supplies.

  • Chicago Board of Trade most-active soybeans were last up 14-3/4 cents to $12.29 per bushel. Most-active corn was last up 4-1/2 cents to $4.65 per bushel.

data.title: Canada Goods-Trade Deficit Widens to C$3.65 Billion — Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--A retreat in Canadian exports of autos, gold and aircraft to start the year drove the country's trade deficit with the world to its widest level in five months in January.

Canada recorded a merchandise-trade shortfall of 3.65 billion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of about US$2.68 billion, Statistics Canada said Thursday. That marks a fourth straight monthly deficit after the country's volatile year for trade following the Trump administration's tariffs.

Economists had expected the trade gap to narrow from December's C$1.3 billion to about a C$1.1 billion deficit for the latest month.

Canada's longstanding surplus with the U.S. narrowed again in January, to the narrowest level since last August, and exports to Canada's biggest market by far continued to decline.

The total value of Canadian goods exports fell 4.7% to C$62.48 billion, while the value of goods coming into the country slipped 1.1% to C$66.13 billion, the national data agency said.

After recovering in December, shipments to the U.S. were down 3.8% for the first month of 2026, and imports from the U.S. declined 3.4%. Canadian exports to countries other than the U.S. dropped 6.5% after hitting a record high in December, while imports from non-U.S. countries increased 2.1%.

The shift saw Canada's longstanding surplus with the U.S. narrow slightly to C$5.4 billion from C$5.75 billion in December. The goods-trade deficit excluding Canada's biggest trading partner widened to C$9.05 billion in January from C$7.05 billion in the prior month.

The Trump administration's isolationist policies and tariffs have weakened Canada's economy at a time when it also faces reduced immigration and an aging population. Economists expect soft momentum in 2026, and the central bank has forecast growth will average about 1.1% for the year.

Activity began 2026 on a soft note, and Statistics Canada has estimated industry-level gross domestic product was essentially unchanged in January from a month earlier. The economy contracted a sharper-than-expected 0.6% on an annualized basis in the final quarter of last year, largely thanks to a big withdrawal from business inventories that offset a recovery in exports, and increases in both household and government spending.

Canadian exports in January fell in six of the 11 product categories tracked by Statistics Canada, and were down 5.8% in volume terms.

Exports of motor vehicles and parts sank 21.2% to the lowest level since September 2021, driven by a steep fall in shipments of cars and light trucks with a drop in production during the month as changes in the models rolling off assembly lines meant prolonged stoppages.

Exports of metals and minerals were down 8% during the month, with unwrought gold again volatile and shipments of the precious metal to the U.K. in particular pulling back.

Shipments of aircraft and other transportation equipment fell 16% following a strong increase in December, with exports to the U.S. down.

Moderating the weak month for exports, Canadian outbound shipments of energy products rose. Exports of natural gas were up strongly with increased prices and volumes, with increased demand from the U.S. as it experienced extreme winter conditions. Crude-oil shipments increased more modestly, though were higher for a third month running.

When international trade in goods and international trade in services were combined, Canadian exports fell 3.9%, while imports slipped 0.4%. As a result, Canada's trade deficit incorporating both goods and services widened to C$3.78 billion from December's C$860 million.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com
